div.div:
Three paper-cutting tricks that build on each other, each more surprising than the last. You need paper strips (about 1 inch wide, 11 inches long), tape or glue, and scissors.
TRICK 1 — THE NORMAL LOOP: Tape a strip into a simple loop. Cut it down the middle lengthwise all the way around. Result: two separate loops. This is the "control" — show this first so the audience knows what to expect.
TRICK 2 — THE MÖBIUS STRIP: Take a new strip, but before taping the ends together, give one end a single half-twist. Now cut down the middle lengthwise. Instead of two loops, you get ONE giant loop twice the size. The audience will not believe it.
TRICK 3 — THE LINKED RINGS: Take a third strip and give it TWO half-twists before taping. Cut down the middle again. This time you get two loops that are linked together like a chain — impossible to separate without tearing. Perform all three in sequence for maximum impact.

The Moebius strip was described mathematically by August Moebius and Johann Listing in 1858 - coincidentally the same year independently. Victorian audience members familiar with geometry were as baffled as non-mathematicians because the result seems to violate intuition.
Cutting a paper ring in half produces impossible results - one large ring, two linked rings, or a twisted Moebius band.
